summary: Julia Evans explores what 'devops' actually means after reading 'Effective DevOps' by Jennifer Davis and Ryn Daniels. She clarifies that devops is fundamentally about communication and collaboration between development and operations teams, not about specific tools like Chef or Puppet. The post distinguishes devops from job titles, automation tooling, and the idea that operations should be made so easy no expertise is needed. Evans reflects on how many devops practices were invisible to her because her organization already followed them, making her realize these were deliberate choices rather than defaults. She concludes that devops is an evolving set of practices the industry is learning together.
key_points: DevOps is about communication and collaboration between development and operations, not about specific tools or job titles
Operations expertise remains essential — devops doesn't mean making operations so easy nobody needs to be an expert
Many devops practices (continuous integration, blameless postmortems) are organizational choices that become invisible when you've never seen the alternative
DevOps is a large, fuzzy, evolving set of practices rather than a fixed manifesto, and organizations can always improve
Operations experts should participate in systems design and architecture, not just be handed finished systems to run
money_quotes: Puppet is software, communication is about how humans work together.
It's not like 'make operations so easy that nobody has to be an expert at it'. Of course you need people who know a ton about operations!
I hadn't realized that this was a choice my organization was making, I thought that was just how things were!
Any organizational choice (like continuous integration, blameless postmortems, having a separate operations team) is a choice, and it's useful to understand why you're making it.

Even if we ostensibly practice 'devops' and do continuous integration and use jenkins and deploy 100 times a day or whatever, that doesn't actually mean that we're the best and awesome and that we can stop.
Devops seems to be less about a 'manifesto' and more of a large and fuzzy set of practices that we're all learning together as an industry over time.
key_insight: DevOps is fundamentally about how people with different expertise collaborate effectively, not about any particular set of tools, and recognizing your organizational practices as deliberate choices opens the door to continuous improvement.
